input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** is a **lab-scale gantry crane**, a **cyber-physical system (CPS)** designed for research and educational purposes. The **physical twin** consists of:
- **Core Components**:
  - A **gantry crane mechanism** (likely a 2D or 3D kinematic structure with movable arms, hooks, or payload carriers).
  - An **embedded controller** (hardware/software system) responsible for real-time motion control, sensor interfacing, and actuation commands.
- **Operational Domain**:
  - **Industrial/academic research** (e.g., testing digital twin synchronization, fault detection, and control optimization).
  - **Kinematic validation** (e.g., trajectory planning, collision avoidance, and dynamic response analysis).
- **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
  - **Reproducibility**: Open-source hardware/software stack with minimal proprietary dependencies.
  - **Modularity**: Designed to integrate with **freely available tools** (e.g., CAD, simulation engines, data logging systems).
  - **Bidirectional Data Flow**: Supports **real-time sensor data acquisition** (e.g., encoder feedback, load cells) and **control command feedback** from the digital twin to the physical crane.
  - **Scalability**: Lab-scale prototype but structurally representative of **industrial gantry cranes** (e.g., similar kinematic constraints, payload limits).
  - **Validation Use Cases**: Previously employed in research on **continuous validation methods**, **fault detection**, and **system evolution notation (DarTwin)**. The crane’s **kinematic model** and **controller** were validated against real-world operation in prior publications.
  - **Physical Specifications (Implied)**:
    - Likely **single-axis or dual-axis** (e.g., X-Y plane movement with hooks or clamps).
    - **Payload capacity** not explicitly stated but assumed to be **low-to-medium** (e.g., <50 kg) for lab use.
    - **Actuation**: Electric motors with **closed-loop control** (e.g., PID or advanced algorithms).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides a **comprehensive suite of services** categorized by function, target users, and technical capabilities. Key services include:

1. **Optimal Control Services**:
   - **Real-time trajectory optimization**: Generates **energy-efficient or time-optimal paths** for the crane’s movement based on kinematic constraints and payload dynamics.
   - **Adaptive control feedback**: Adjusts control parameters dynamically using **model predictive control (MPC)** or **reinforcement learning** (implied by validation use cases).
   - **Target Users**: Researchers/developers testing **advanced control algorithms** or **human-in-the-loop validation**.

2. **Monitoring and Validation Services**:
   - **Continuous validation**: Implements **real-time consistency checks** between the **physical crane’s sensor data** (e.g., encoder positions, accelerometer readings) and the **digital twin’s kinematic model**. Uses **residual analysis** or **statistical process control (SPC)** to detect discrepancies.
   - **Fault detection**: Detects **anomalies** (e.g., motor stalls, sensor drift, structural fatigue) via **machine learning models** or **rule-based thresholds**. Validated in prior work ([6]).
   - **Historical data logging**: Stores **time-series data** (e.g., position, velocity, torque) for **post-mortem analysis**, **performance benchmarking**, or **retrospective validation**.
   - **Target Users**: Maintenance engineers, reliability analysts, and **digital twin researchers**.

3. **Data Visualization Services**:
   - **3D/2D kinematic visualization**: Renders the **physical crane’s state** (e.g., real-time hook position, velocity profiles) using **CAD models** or **graphical interfaces** (e.g., MATLAB, Python-based tools).
   - **Comparative analytics**: Overlays **physical vs. digital twin trajectories** to highlight deviations (e.g., due to friction, wind, or control errors).
   - **Target Users**: Educators, students, and **visualization-focused researchers**.

4. **Simulation and Prediction Services**:
   - **Kinematic simulation**: Replays or predicts **future states** of the crane under **hypothetical conditions** (e.g., payload changes, environmental disturbances).
   - **What-if analysis**: Simulates **failure scenarios** (e.g., motor failure, load imbalance) to assess **system robustness**.
   - **Target Users**: System designers, safety engineers, and **digital twin theorists**.

5. **System Evolution Services**:
   - **DarTwin notation support**: Enables **formal modeling of system evolution** (e.g., hardware/software updates, configuration changes) using a **notation framework** ([7]) to track **digital twin synchronization** over time.
   - **Target Users**: Digital twin architects and **systems engineering researchers**.

**Technical Implementation Notes**:
- Services are **service-oriented** (likely RESTful APIs or microservices) to enable **plug-and-play integration** with other systems.
- **Data synchronization** occurs via **bidirectional communication protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, or custom TCP/IP streams).
- **Latency requirements**: Implied to be **low (<100ms)** for real-time control validation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin** is implemented using a **stack of open-source and industry-standard tools**, ensuring **reproducibility** and **interoperability**. Key enablers include:

1. **CAD and Kinematic Modeling**:
   - **CAD Tools**: Likely **FreeCAD**, **OpenSCAD**, or **Blender** for **3D modeling** of the crane’s geometry.
   - **Kinematic Libraries**: **Python (SymPy, SciPy)** or **MATLAB/Simulink** for **forward/inverse kinematics**, **Jacobian calculations**, and **dynamics simulations**.
   - **Digital Twin Core**: A **custom Python-based framework** (or MATLAB-based) integrating:
     - **State estimation** (e.g., Kalman filters for sensor fusion).
     - **Control algorithms** (e.g., PID, MPC, or learned controllers).
     - **Validation logic** (e.g., residual generation for fault detection).

2. **Simulation Engines**:
   - **Real-time simulation**: **ROS (Robot Operating System)** or **Gazebo** for **physics-based simulation** of the crane’s dynamics (if applicable).
   - **Offline analysis**: **Python (NumPy, Pandas)** or **MATLAB** for **historical data processing** and **statistical validation**.

3. **Data Storage and Processing**:
   - **Databases**: **SQLite**, **PostgreSQL**, or **InfluxDB** for **time-series data logging** (e.g., storing sensor readings, control commands).
   - **Streaming Protocols**: **MQTT** or **OPC UA** for **real-time data ingestion** from the physical crane.
   - **Data Processing**: **Apache Spark** or **Dask** for **large-scale historical analysis** (if scaling beyond lab use).

4. **Visualization Platforms**:
   - **Interactive Dashboards**: **Plotly Dash**, **Dash Enterprise**, or **MATLAB’s App Designer** for **real-time monitoring**.
   - **3D Rendering**: **Unity**, **Unreal Engine**, or **Blender** for **immersive visualization** of the digital twin’s state.

5. **Development Tools**:
   - **Programming Languages**: **Python (primary)**, **MATLAB**, or **C++** for **low-latency control loops**.
   - **Version Control**: **Git** for **collaborative development** of the digital twin’s codebase.
   - **CI/CD**: **GitHub Actions** or **Jenkins** for **automated testing** of validation methods.

6. **Specialized Technologies**:
   - **Fault Detection**: **Python libraries** (e.g., `scikit-learn`, `TensorFlow`) for **anomaly detection models**.
   - **Continuous Validation**: **Custom algorithms** (e.g., **residual generation**, **statistical hypothesis testing**) implemented in Python/MATLAB.
   - **DarTwin Notation**: A **formal modeling language** ([7]) for **documenting system evolution**, likely integrated via **graph-based tools** (e.g., **Graphviz**, **yEd**).

7. **Hardware Interfacing**:
   - **Sensor Integration**: **Arduino**, **Raspberry Pi**, or **NI LabVIEW** for **data acquisition** from encoders, load cells, and IMUs.
   - **Actuator Control**: **PWM signals** or **CAN bus** for **motor command generation**.

8. **Open-Source Principles**:
   - **No proprietary software dependencies**: Entire stack relies on **freely available tools** (e.g., ROS, Python, MATLAB’s open-source alternatives).
   - **Modular Design**: Components (e.g., kinematic model, fault detector) are **independently testable** and **reusable** across projects.
   - **Public Availability**: The **physical crane and digital twin code** are **openly accessible** (e.g., via GitHub repositories or academic datasets), enabling **community-driven improvements**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided exemplars do not explicitly detail multiplicities or hierarchical structures. However, the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs a **single digital twin instance** per physical system (the greenhouse) with **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical and digital components, suggesting a **one-to-one mapping** between the physical system and its digital twin. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar also appears to utilize a **single digital twin instance** per indoor air quality management system, with no explicit mention of multiple instances or decentralized architectures. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is described using the **C4 Model**, but no details on multiplicities (e.g., multiple crane twins or hierarchical coordination) are provided in the documents. All exemplars rely on **ad-hoc approaches** rather than standardized frameworks, leaving no explicit documentation of coordination mechanisms between multiple twins or decentralized responsibilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin systems in the provided documents employ **ad-hoc orchestration and coordination** rather than commercial frameworks. **GreenhouseDT** uses **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage, **ActiveMQ** for message brokering, and **virtual machines** with pre-recorded data for synchronization, indicating a **centralized data pipeline** with predefined temporal alignment. **Air Quality Management** leverages **AutomationML-based digital twins** and **Microsoft Azure tools** for runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction, suggesting a **cloud-centric orchestration** with Azure services managing coordination. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is described using the **C4 Model**, but no specific orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) are detailed. All systems rely on **containerization or virtualization** (e.g., virtual machines, Raspberry Pi-based setups) for deployment, with **no explicit mention of resource management policies** or system-wide coordination mechanisms beyond ad-hoc integration of tools like InfluxDB and ActiveMQ.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar integrates with **InfluxDB** (time-series database) and **ActiveMQ** (message broker) for data exchange, relying on **pre-recorded data** in virtual machines. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar integrates with **Raspberry Pi sensors** (physical layer) and **Microsoft Azure** (cloud layer) via **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, with no explicit mention of interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, IoT protocols). The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system is described using the **C4 Model**, but no external system integrations or protocols are documented. All exemplars use **ad-hoc integration patterns** with minimal reliance on standardized interoperability frameworks, and there is **no evidence of enterprise system integration** (e.g., ERP, MES) or cross-twin communication in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system for the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar employs **behavioral adaptation** of both the physical system (greenhouse) and its digital twin, utilizing **InfluxDB** for time-series data storage and **ActiveMQ** for message brokerage to facilitate real-time data exchange. The system integrates **prerecorded data** within virtual machine images for ease of deployment, enabling **geometric (structural layout) and behavioral (environmental control) models** to be dynamically updated. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar uses **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, combining **simulation, prediction, and runtime visualization** capabilities. Data is sourced from a **Raspberry Pi-based sensor network** and processed via **Microsoft Azure tools**, supporting **analytical models** for indoor air quality prediction. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar (mentioned in context) lacks explicit model/data details in the provided documents, but the **C4 Model** is referenced for architectural visualization, implying structured **geometric (crane mechanics) and behavioral (movement control) models** with potential **containerized deployment** for scalability. Model relationships are implied through **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital components, though specific data management approaches (e.g., versioning, reconciliation) are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **Air Quality Management** exemplar employs a **Raspberry Pi** as the primary sensing infrastructure, equipped with a **set of sensors** for monitoring indoor air quality parameters (e.g., particulate matter, CO₂, humidity). The system lacks explicit details on **spatial distribution**, **sampling frequencies**, or **data transmission rates**, but the use of **Raspberry Pi** implies moderate computational capabilities for on-board processing. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar does not explicitly describe sensing components in the provided documents, though its reliance on **InfluxDB** suggests time-series data collection from environmental sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity) via unspecified interfaces. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar (contextual) is not detailed for sensing components in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar emphasizes **self-adaptive** behavior for both physical and digital systems, implying **dynamic model validation** through real-time feedback loops. However, specific **uncertainty quantification**, **error detection mechanisms**, or **quality assurance processes** are not mentioned. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar focuses on **Model-Driven Engineering**, suggesting **formal validation** via AutomationML-based simulations and Azure-driven predictions, though **uncertainty sources** (e.g., sensor noise, model bias) and **correction mechanisms** are not detailed. The **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** exemplar (contextual) lacks explicit fidelity considerations in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in the lab-scale gantry crane Digital Twin system involves real-time data acquisition from the **Physical Crane** hardware, processed through the **Physical Crane Interface** (a Python program) to ensure seamless integration with the digital components. Key data flows include:
- **Data Types Transmitted**: Sensor measurements (e.g., position, velocity, acceleration of the crane and pucks), controller status signals, and execution logs.
- **Communication Protocols**: Direct communication between the **Physical Crane Interface** and the crane controllers, with no explicit protocol specified beyond Python-based connectivity.
- **Transmission Frequencies**: Continuous logging of crane state via the **Logging Application**, though exact frequency is unspecified; implied to be high-frequency for real-time monitoring.
- **Event Triggers**: Data acquisition is event-driven (e.g., trajectory completion, operator commands) and time-driven (continuous logging to **TimescaleDB**).
- **Data Quality Assurance**: No explicit mention of validation filters or preprocessing steps, though the **Continuous Validation Service** later compares simulated vs. executed trajectories for post-hoc validation.
- **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Data is ingested into the **TimescaleDB** database via the **Logging Application**, which acts as a centralized repository for all system-generated data. The **MQTT Broker** facilitates signaling between services but is not explicitly tied to physical data ingestion.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this system is mediated by the **Control Application**, which generates and executes commands for the **Physical Crane** via the **Physical Crane Interface**. Key components include:
- **Command Types**: Movement trajectories (optimized via the **Trajectory Generator Service**) and control signals for crane actuators, designed to eliminate residual swinging motion.
- **Control Message Formats**: Commands are transmitted as structured inputs to the **Physical Crane Interface**, though exact formats (e.g., JSON, binary) are unspecified. The **Trajectory Generator Service** uses kinematic models to produce trajectory data.
- **Validation and Authentication**: No explicit authentication mechanisms are described, but the **Continuous Validation Service** validates executed trajectories against simulations, triggering alerts via the **Dashboard** if deviations exceed thresholds.
- **Feedback Systems**: The **Logging Application** captures real-time state data, which is later compared to simulated trajectories in the **Continuous Validation Service** to ensure alignment between virtual and physical behavior.
- **Conflict Resolution**: Not explicitly detailed, but the **Dashboard** likely provides operator intervention points for manual overrides or corrective actions.
- **Command Execution Confirmation**: Implied via the **Continuous Validation Service**, which verifies trajectory execution against simulated expectations and notifies operators of discrepancies via the **Dashboard**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** infrastructure relies on an **MQTT Broker** for inter-service communication, ensuring low-latency signaling between containers (e.g., **Trajectory Generator Service**, **Simulation Service**, **Continuous Validation Service**). Key details include:
- **Communication Protocols**: MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for service-to-service signaling, enabling asynchronous, lightweight messaging.
- **Network Topology and Architecture**: Centralized architecture with the **MQTT Broker** acting as a hub for all digital twin services, connected to the **TimescaleDB** database and **Dashboard**. No explicit mention of distributed or edge deployment.
- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**: Not quantified, but implied to be low-latency due to real-time trajectory validation and operator monitoring requirements.
- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**: No redundancy or failover mechanisms are described; the system assumes operational continuity of the **MQTT Broker** and database.
- **Security Protocols**: Not mentioned; no encryption, authentication, or access control mechanisms are specified for network communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed beyond containerized services (using the **C4 Model** abstraction, not Docker). Key inferred details include:
- **Hosting Platforms**: Likely **on-premise** deployment given the lab-scale context and lack of cloud/edge references. The **TimescaleDB** and **Grafana Dashboard** are deployed as part of the system’s backend.
- **Computational and Storage Requirements**: Not specified, but the **TimescaleDB** suggests high-volume time-series data storage, and the **Simulation Service** implies computational resources for kinematic modeling and trajectory validation.
- **Scalability Mechanisms**: No scalability features (e.g., auto-scaling, load balancing) are mentioned; the system is designed for a single gantry crane instance.
- **Deployment Models Supported**: Implicitly supports **monolithic deployment** of all digital twin services (database, services, dashboard) as a cohesive unit, with no mention of microservices or container orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes).
- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: No redundancy or high-availability mechanisms are described; reliability depends on the stability of the **MQTT Broker**, database, and physical crane interface.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin exemplars described in the documents employ an **ad-hoc development approach** rather than standardized digital twin frameworks. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar uses **virtual machines (VMs)** preloaded with prerecorded data, **InfluxDB** for time-series database storage, and **ActiveMQ** for message brokering to facilitate bidirectional data exchange between the physical greenhouse and its digital twin. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar leverages **Raspberry Pi** with embedded sensors for real-time data acquisition, **AutomationML** for digital twin representation, and **Microsoft Azure** tools for pipeline implementation. Version control is managed via **GitHub repositories** ([GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT), [Air Quality Management](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)), ensuring traceability of code and configuration changes. Evolution management is implicit in the modular design, where **containerization** (e.g., Docker) and **virtualization** (e.g., VMs) enable scalable updates and adaptations. Quality assurance practices are not explicitly detailed, but the use of **prerecorded data** in GreenhouseDT suggests offline validation. Testing and validation are likely domain-specific, relying on sensor calibration (e.g., Raspberry Pi sensors) and simulation outputs (e.g., Air Quality Management’s prediction capabilities).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Air Quality Management** exemplar provides **three key decision-support applications**:
  1. **Runtime visualization** of indoor air quality metrics via AutomationML-based digital twin representations.
  2. **Simulation** of air flow dynamics and pollutant dispersion using Azure-based tools.
  3. **Prediction** of virus spread risks via analytics pipelines, though specific algorithms are not detailed.
  User interaction occurs through **Azure-hosted interfaces**, and confidence measures are implied by the system’s reliance on **real-time sensor data** (Raspberry Pi) and **prerecorded calibration datasets**. The **GreenhouseDT** exemplar lacks explicit decision-making capabilities but enables **behavioral adaptation** of the physical greenhouse via **ActiveMQ-mediated feedback loops**, suggesting adaptive control based on twin-simulated insights. No confidence metrics or user interaction methods are specified for GreenhouseDT.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The exemplars exhibit **limited standardization**:
  - **GreenhouseDT** adheres to **InfluxDB** (time-series database standard) and **ActiveMQ** (JMS-compliant message broker), but no industry-specific DT standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 23247) are referenced.
  - **Air Quality Management** uses **AutomationML** (ISO 15926-11 compliant) for digital twin representation and **Microsoft Azure** (cloud interoperability standards), but lacks formal certification or compliance frameworks.
  - **No interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, PLCopen) are explicitly adopted. The **lab-scale gantry crane** (mentioned in discussion) is described using the **C4 Model** for architecture visualization, but this is a **software design methodology**, not a DT standard. Overall, the exemplars follow **ad-hoc technical stacks** without adherence to widely recognized DT frameworks or certifications.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents do not explicitly detail security and safety measures for the **GreenhouseDT** or **Air Quality Management** exemplars. However, the **Lab Scale Gantry Crane** system (while not fully described in the provided documents) is implied to require robust cybersecurity protocols due to its cyber-physical nature. For the **GreenhouseDT** exemplar, no specific security frameworks (e.g., OAuth, TLS, or role-based access control) are mentioned, nor are there references to fail-safe mechanisms or threat mitigation strategies. The **Air Quality Management** exemplar relies on **Microsoft Azure tools**, which inherently include security features such as Azure Active Directory for authentication, role-based access control (RBAC), and encryption (e.g., TLS for data in transit). However, the documents do not specify compliance with regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, or industry-specific standards (e.g., ISO 27001). No consent management, data anonymization, or explicit data-sharing agreements are documented. The **ad-hoc approach** of these exemplars suggests minimal formalized governance, though the use of **Raspberry Pi** and **InfluxDB** implies some level of data isolation via containerization (e.g., Docker) or virtualization (e.g., VMs), which could theoretically enforce access controls. No safety-critical system considerations (e.g., fail-safes for sensor failures or network partitions) are described in the provided context.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give
block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
